data.title: Cleveland-Cliffs projects $575M Q3 adjusted EBITDA while targeting sub 2.5x leverage by next year
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Cleveland-Cliffs (CLF) Q2 2026

Management view

  • "After several quarters of talking about the future earnings power of this company, we can finally point to tangible evidence that the progression we have been forecasting is now reality," said Chairman, President & CEO Lourenco Goncalves. "During the second quarter, we returned to positive free cash flow and tripled our adjusted EBITDA from the first quarter."
  • "That will be more visible in Q3, in which we are expecting to more than double our Q2 EBITDA," said Chairman, President & CEO Goncalves, adding, "With our third quarter adjusted EBITDA guidance of $575 million, we have a situation where higher prices, lower costs and higher shipping volumes will all be converging at once."
  • "One important item to mention today, we have officially kicked off negotiations with the United Steel Workers Union to renew our collective bargain agreement," said Chairman, President & CEO Goncalves. "And I'm pleased to say that the process is off to a constructive and productive start."
  • "Before turning it over, I would like to recognize Celso's appointment to our Board of Directors as President and CFO that was announced this morning," said Chairman, President & CEO Goncalves. "It also marks the early stages of a transition in leadership. I'm not going anywhere anytime soon."
  • "Our adjusted EBITDA in the second quarter was $286 million, our best quarter in 2 years," said Executive VP & CFO Celso Goncalves. "Second quarter shipments were just over 4 million tons, down sequentially from the previous quarter due to the maintenance outages we underwent during the quarter."
  • "Our average selling price increased by $76 per ton," said Executive VP & CFO Goncalves. "Based on this, we expect our average price to increase another $55 per ton in Q3."

Outlook

  • "We expect adjusted EBITDA of approximately $575 million in the third quarter, which would be our strongest quarter in 3 years," said Executive VP & CFO Goncalves. "With where the curve for HRC stands today, we would expect even further improvement on that figure in the fourth quarter, even with the typical seasonal slowdown we usually see around the holidays."
  • "We'll have an opportunity in the coming months to reset a large portion of our fixed price contracts substantially higher," said Executive VP & CFO Goncalves. "[W]e estimate [this] will represent a $500 million EBITDA improvement year-over-year."
  • Compared with last quarter’s framing that Q2 would be the best quarter in nearly two years and Q3 would show the earnings power more fully, management’s guidance language shifted to explicit quarterly targets and a stronger conviction statement. "Cleveland-Cliffs is entering the strongest earnings environment that we have seen in years," said Executive VP & CFO Goncalves.

Financial results

  • "Our adjusted EBITDA in the second quarter was $286 million," said Executive VP & CFO Goncalves, and "after 2 years of negative free cash flow, we finally flipped back to positive in the second quarter."
  • "Second quarter shipments were just over 4 million tons," said Executive VP & CFO Goncalves, and "we expect to see steel shipment volumes above 4.3 million tons in the third quarter as the order book remains strong and backlogs are extended."
  • "As for unit costs, as previously guided, the inventory lag effect from last quarter and our maintenance outages pushed costs up quarter-over-quarter," said Executive VP & CFO Goncalves. "But with that behind us, we should see a $10 per ton reduction in costs into Q3."
  • "[W]e are now under contract on all of our major property sales with earnest money in our control in all cases," said Executive VP & CFO Goncalves. "The bulk of the $400 million proceeds from our property sales are expected to come in the second half of this year."

Q&A

  • Carlos de Alba, Morgan Stanley: asked about timing and scope of resetting non-auto fixed-price contracts and expectations for auto contract resets; Chairman, President & CEO Goncalves responded, "it's a process that starts in earnest in the second half of the of this year," and on auto pricing, "we are going to reset the numbers higher."
  • Carlos de Alba, Morgan Stanley: asked about Q4 cost direction; Chairman, President & CEO Goncalves answered, "Yes, we expect further improvements," citing "more optimized schedules at the mills, thanks to our work with Palantir."
  • Samuel McKinney, KeyBanc: asked how much of the Q3 shipment uplift is automotive-driven; Chairman, President & CEO Goncalves said, "I would say half." McKinney also pressed on payables’ impact on free cash flow; Executive VP & CFO Goncalves said, "The payables were largely driven by things like raw materials going up, additional maintenance work and things like that."
  • Nicklaus Cash, Goldman Sachs: asked for Stelco pricing color and the $500 million uplift bridge; Chairman, President & CEO Goncalves said, "the pricing gap has closed," but "we haven't seen yet the same type of impact with the galvanized steel." He added, "if I need to make any changes in the Canadian footprint, it will be all affecting galvanized and producing more hot rolled."
  • Nick Giles, B. Riley: asked about capacity restarts and Dearborn; Chairman, President & CEO Goncalves said restarting hinges on customers’ commitment, adding, "I need the conviction" and "we're not going to go until they are ready to go." Giles also asked about debt paydown; Executive VP & CFO Goncalves said, "debt paydown is going to be our #1 capital allocation priority," and "we don't have anything maturing until 2029."
  • Richard Garchitorena, Barclays: asked what drives the expectation that Q4 outperforms Q3 despite seasonality; Chairman, President & CEO Goncalves emphasized contracting visibility, saying, "we have a good visibility into volumes" and "we already baked in" holiday shipment reductions.

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly skeptical, concentrating on contract reset mechanics, free-cash-flow quality, Canada/Stelco profitability, and the durability of Q4 improvement; questions repeatedly pressed for bridges and timing (e.g., "what's driving the incremental improvement" from Barclays).
  • Management’s tone was positive and confident in prepared remarks and remained forceful in Q&A, with repeated conviction language such as "we have a good visibility" and "we are going to reset the numbers higher," while also signaling discipline on transactions: "we're not desperate to do anything," said Executive VP & CFO Goncalves.
  • Versus last quarter’s emphasis on one-timers and normalization, management’s tone shifted toward higher certainty and explicit targets, including "we are expecting to more than double our Q2 EBITDA" and "we expect adjusted EBITDA of approximately $575 million in the third quarter."

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance posture tightened from Q1’s directional setup ("Q2 should be our best quarter in nearly 2 years" and "Q3 should be much better than Q2," said Executive VP & CFO Goncalves last quarter) to Q2’s explicit quarterly adjusted EBITDA guide for Q3 and stated expectation for Q4 improvement.
  • Strategic emphasis expanded from Q1’s operational normalization, outages, and AI-partner announcement timing to Q2’s broader stance on valuation and deal pressure, with Executive VP & CFO Goncalves saying counterparties assumed Cliffs "was under pressure to transact" and that offers "have fallen short of our value threshold."
  • Analyst focus stayed consistent on contracts, costs, and cash generation, but Q2 added more probing on Stelco’s pricing mix and the conditions for restarting capacity.

Risks and concerns

  • Canada exposure remained a named risk: "Without further measures to protect fair trade in Canada, the future competitiveness of our galvanizing lines in Hamilton is at risk," said Chairman, President & CEO Goncalves.
  • Transaction uncertainty was framed as optionality and discipline: "discussions still remain friendly and ongoing" with POSCO, but "valuation and structure are important," said Executive VP & CFO Goncalves.
  • Labor negotiations were highlighted as underway, with management stating, "we are confident that we can reach an agreement," said Chairman, President & CEO Goncalves.

Final takeaway

Management described a shift from recovery setup to visible acceleration, anchored by Q2’s $286 million adjusted EBITDA, a return to positive free cash flow, and Q3 adjusted EBITDA guidance of approximately $575 million. Executives tied the step-up to pricing realization, higher volumes, and cost reductions, while emphasizing balance-sheet deleveraging as the top priority and signaling transaction discipline on potential asset or strategic deals, alongside ongoing labor negotiations and continued scrutiny of Canadian trade protections affecting Stelco’s finishing operations.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Teck Resources rises as Q2 profit triples on record copper prices
data.description:
Teck Resources (TECK) up 3.7% in Thursday's trading after reporting Q2 profit tripled from a year ago on the back of higher copper prices, while reaffirming full-year production guidance.

Q2 adjusted EBITDA surged to C$2.19B (~US$1.6B) from C$722M in the year-earlier quarter, while revenue rose 78% Y/Y to C$3.61B, as benchmark three-month copper prices on the London Metals Exchange jumped 41% Y/Y, powered by concerns over tight supplies and strong demand in China.

Teck (TECK) said its realized copper prices averaged $6.05/lb in Q2, up from $4.32/lb in the prior-year period, and production climbed 24.6% to 135.9K tons.

Copper production at the Quebrada Blanca mine in Chile totaled 55.8K tons, slightly higher than both Q1 and the year-ago quarter, the company said, maintaining full-year output guidance for the mine at 200K-235K tons.

Overall, the company maintained full-year guidance for copper production of 455K-530K metric tons and zinc production of 410K-460K tons, which would deliver refined zinc of 190K-230K tons.

"Operationally, we continue to increase momentum across the business," Teck (TECK) President and CEO Jonathan Price said on the company's earnings conference call, noting that Quebrada Blanca achieved a third consecutive quarter of stable operating performance.

Teck (TECK), which is in the process of merging ​with Anglo American (AAUKF) (NGLOY), said the merger approval process with Chinese regulators ​is progressing normally and that it has not received any requests for remedies ​from authorities in China.

Teck (TECK) also said it is continuing to evaluate a broad range of ‌potential feedstock ⁠sources for germanium processing as it works to expand its capabilities in the mineral; the company is North America's largest germanium producer.

data.title: First American targets Endpoint scale by end of 2027 while expanding Sequoia toward 70% purchase automation
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: First American Financial Corporation (FAF) Q2 2026

Management View

  • "Our earnings momentum continued in the second quarter as we generated adjusted earnings per share of $2.08, an increase of 36% from the prior year," said CEO Mark Seaton, highlighting "Commercial continued to be a standout performer" with "Revenue increased 34%, setting a second quarter record" and "We closed 14 transactions, generating more than $1 million of premium."

  • Seaton positioned First American Trust as a key earnings lever, saying, "One of the most important earnings drivers continues to be our bank" and noting "average deposits totaled $7.9 billion" with "36% of deposits" from outside captive title operations; he added that ServiceMac "accounted for $1.7 billion of deposits" and that "36%" and the deposit sources "provide meaningful long-term growth opportunities while reinforcing the bank's role as a valuable countercyclical earnings driver."

  • Seaton framed AI execution as the primary strategy, saying, "Our primary strategic priority remains leveraging AI across the enterprise" and citing operational and product examples including "we reduced the time required by 97%" for updating "1,300 forms," and that Exam Assist QC "has now processed more than 50,000 orders, delivering 92% with no additional human review."

  • CFO Matthew Wajner summarized results, saying, "This quarter, we generated GAAP earnings of $2.12 per diluted share" and "adjusted total revenue was $2 billion, up 14%" in the title segment, while commercial revenue was "$314 million" and purchase and refinance trends reflected housing affordability and rate-driven volatility.

Outlook

  • Seaton said, "We remain optimistic about our earnings trajectory for the second half of the year," reiterating, "we continue to believe" commercial is "on pace to deliver a record year" and adding, "Our commercial pipeline has never been stronger" with "commercial open orders" up "9%" over the first "3 weeks" of July.

  • On residential purchase, Seaton stated, "We remain more cautious than the broader consensus" and said, "Through the first 3 weeks of July, our open purchase orders are flat relative to last year as existing home sales remained sluggish."

  • On platform deployment timelines and targets, Seaton said Endpoint is expected to scale "across our local title branch network by the end of 2027" and that Sequoia aims over time to "automate title decisioning for approximately 70% of purchase transactions and 80% of refinanced transactions" in relevant markets, with Sequoia expected "by year-end" to be "deployed across California and Florida" and a "broader national rollout planned for 2027."

Financial Results

  • Wajner reported title segment details including "Commercial revenue was $314 million" and "Average revenue per order was $19,980 per transaction," while purchase revenue increased "2%" and refinance revenue increased "18%"; he also said "Refinance accounted for just 5% of our direct revenue" in the quarter.

  • Wajner said "Investment income was $164 million" and "Interest expense was $30 million," and described cost trends including "Personnel costs were $572 million" and "Other operating expenses were $319 million," while noting the success ratio was "66%" and tied it to "investments" such as ServiceMac.

  • Capital and balance sheet items included Wajner’s statement that the company repurchased "330,000 shares" for "$20 million" at an "average price of $61.99," and that debt-to-capital was "31.4%" ("21.5%" excluding secured financings payable).

Q&A

  • Terry Ma, Barclays: asked about deposit growth sustainability and investment income cadence; CEO Mark Seaton said, "we have a bank, and it's a real strategic advantage for us" and described third-party deposit initiatives, while CFO Wajner said, "investment income net of interest expense grew 8% year-over-year" and added, "I think that 8% is a good proxy for the growth that you'll see in the back half of the year."

  • Terry Ma, Barclays: asked about commercial ARPO durability; CEO Seaton replied, "We think ARPO continue to grow in the second half of the year" and said the company feels it is "still in the early innings of the next commercial real estate cycle."

  • Oscar Nieves Santana, Stephens: asked about title margin trajectory and expense efficiency; CFO Wajner said title segment margin year-to-date was "12.3%" and added expansion depends on commercial and is "hard to forecast," while explaining the "66%" success ratio was influenced by ServiceMac investments.

  • Oscar Nieves Santana, Stephens: asked about buyback pace and debt-to-capital; CEO Seaton said repurchases are "opportunistic" and added, "The debt to cap doesn't really play into that now," while noting, "Our target debt to cap is 20%, and we're a little bit higher than that now."

  • Bose George, KBW: asked why purchase ARPO rose and requested commercial asset class mix plus regulatory update; CFO Wajner attributed purchase ARPO to "geographic mix, particularly California," CEO Seaton said industrial was "23%" of premium and that "data center revenue is up 140% -- 147%" year-over-year, and on the FHFA-related post he said, "Haven't heard anything incremental about that."

  • Mark DeVries, Deutsche Bank: asked about which asset classes are growing and data center premium dynamics; CEO Seaton cited development sites up "33%," multifamily up "23%," retail up "59%" and said data centers involve "$1 billion deals" with premiums that can be "$1 million-plus," while adding he was "not really sure" how the three-step premium sizes rank.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly skeptical, concentrating on durability of commercial pricing/ARPO, margin sustainability, capital return pacing, and regulatory risk; questions repeatedly pressed on sustainability and forecasting difficulty, including concerns implied by asking "how sustainable" deposit inflows and ARPO increases are and whether there was "anything incremental" on FHFA.

  • Management tone was slightly positive and confident, emphasizing execution and long-run platforms, with Seaton stating, "We remain optimistic" and "Our commercial pipeline has never been stronger," while also using measured language on residential purchase markets and regulatory items such as "wait-and-see mode."

  • Versus the prior quarter, management maintained similar confidence on commercial and AI rollout timelines, while analysts in Q2 leaned more into margin mechanics, bank deposit sustainability, and the FHFA-related headline risk rather than broader AI-disruption framing.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Commercial remained the central growth narrative, shifting from Q1’s emphasis on record first-quarter performance and energy/data center tailwinds to Q2’s focus on record second-quarter revenue, larger-deal premium contribution, and broader asset-class strength (including Seaton’s Q2 claim that "10 of our 11 asset classes" grew year-over-year).

  • Residential purchase commentary stayed cautious in both quarters, but the near-term datapoint moved from Q1 open purchase orders down "3%" in early April to Q2 open purchase orders "flat" in the first three weeks of July.

  • The bank and deposits grew in prominence, with Q2 highlighting higher average deposits and a larger share from non-captive sources than Q1, and Q2 Q&A drilling into sustainability and net investment income trends.

  • Platform execution advanced from Q1 pilots and early automation to Q2’s first local title office conversion in Spokane and higher reported automation rates and broader Sequoia county footprint.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited macro sensitivity in residential activity, with Seaton noting "affordability challenges" and that refinance volumes "have moderated" as rates moved higher; Wajner also described refinance as "challenged" versus historical levels.

  • Expense and investment tradeoffs were flagged through Wajner’s explanation that the success ratio ran above target due to growth investments, and through the pairing of higher investment income with higher interest expense as deposits expand.

  • Regulatory uncertainty surfaced in Q&A, with Seaton stating, "Haven't heard anything incremental" regarding the FHFA-related commentary, leaving timing and scope unclear.

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q2 as a continuation of earnings momentum led by commercial pricing and large-transaction activity, while positioning First American Trust deposit growth as a durable, countercyclical contributor to investment income. The second-half outlook leaned on a “never been stronger” commercial pipeline, continued caution on residential purchase demand, and accelerating AI-led operational changes, including scaling Endpoint through 2027 and expanding Sequoia toward long-run automation targets across key states ahead of a planned national rollout in 2027.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Hims & Hers gains as FDA panel endorses peptide BPC-157
data.description:
Shares of Hims & Hers Health (HIMS) rose more than 10% on Thursday after an FDA advisory panel ​voted to include a popular peptide known as ​BPC-157 in a list of molecules that compounding pharmacies use to make customized treatments.

The FDA’s pharmacy compounding advisory committee voted 8–6 to place BPC-157 free base form on the agency’s 503A Bulks List and voted 8–6 with one abstention to place BPC-157 Acetate form on the same list.

Compounding pharmacies are allowed to use constituents of the 503A Bulks List for the production of customized treatments.

The voting comes on the first day of a two-day meeting scheduled to discuss seven peptide products that the agency removed in 2023 from the list.

The FDA's advisory committees issue non-binding recommendations. However, the regulator usually follows them before making a final decision on authorizations.

Hims & Hers Health (HIMS), which markets compounding drugs, acquired a peptide facility in California last year. The company is poised to capture a fifth of the telehealth peptide market, expected to reach $2.2B in 2027, according to Leerink Partners.

data.title: Arista Networks sees largely bullish views at BofA ahead of Q2 results
data.description:
BofA maintained Arista Networks' (ANET) Buy rating and $200 price objective ahead of the company's second quarter results on Aug. 4, citing its "expanding AI opportunity, Cloud Titan strength, and growing campus presence." However, the firm also highlighted margin pressure.

Shares of Arista climbed about 1% on Thursday.

"The stock is up 33% YTD versus NASDAQ up 11%, driven by accelerating AI networking demand. We focus on three factors into the print: (1) progress toward the $3.5bn AI fabric revenue target for 2026 (2) deferred revenue trends amid customers’ completion of DC buildouts, and (3) gross margin pressure from Cloud Titan mix and higher component costs," said analysts led by Tal Liani.

Big tech hyperscale data center operators are also called as Cloud Titans by some.

The analysts noted that they have modeled the second quarter revenue growth of 27% year-over-year, with a high bar of investor expectations for the full year 2026, modeled at 35% growth versus management's 28% guidance.

"Gross margin remains a key near-term swing factor as higher Cloud Titan mix and component costs offset strong revenue growth. We model 2Q gross margin of 62.5%, reflecting ~300bps of YoY pressure," said Liani and his team.

data.title: Galderma Group AG Non-GAAP EPS of $2.34, net sales of $3.13B
